channel.title: Beginner's Guide to Group Field Theory
channel.description: Beginner's Guide to Group Field Theory

item.title: 3: The Lorentz Connection
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 The algebra

This post will begin the journey from pure group theory towards a connection with the universe we are creating. The name ‘Lorentz connection’ is a bit of a pun, as we will be looking at how connects to the Lorentz algebra of symmetries in special relativity, but we won’t actually be building any spin connections or other explicit geometric structures in this post.

To start with, let’s remind ourselves of the algebra . This was given in the first post as the algebra with generators , where and run from 0 to 4, and commutation relations

where . We will now connect this algebra back to the familiar maths of special relativity.

To start with, we will do a simple relabelling of the generators. Let’s define indices and that run from 0 to 3, and define and , where is a constant with dimensions of length. We can now rewrite the commutation relations in terms of these new generators:

Remember this is just a relabelling, none of the symmetries of the group have changed. But now we have some suggestive-shaped algebra: the first line is the algebra of Lorentz transformations, , and the second line says that the transform as a four-vector under Lorentz transformations. This line is also familiar from the Poincaré algebra of special relativity. The third line, however, is different from special relativity: it says that two translations do not commute, but instead give a rotation. This is a key feature of the algebra , and it will have important consequences for the physics that emerges from this algebra. We write the separation of the algebra into these two parts as , where is the part of the algebra generated by the . The group corresponding to the algebra is called the de Sitter group, and is represented as the quotient group .

We can think of the non-commuting of translations like the behaviour of translations on a sphere: if you start at the north pole and translate down to the equator, then translate along the equator, and then translate back up to the north pole, you will be facing in a different direction than you started. This is a consequence of the curvature of the sphere, and it implies that the universe we are building also has a built-in curvature.

If we take the limit , the commutation relations reduce to those of the Poincaré algebra, which is the algebra of symmetries of flat spacetime in special relativity. This means that the algebra can be thought of as a ‘deformation’ of the Poincaré algebra, where the deformation parameter is .

2 Casimir invariants in our new basis

The Casimir invariants of can also be rewritten in terms of the new generators. Recall that there is a second order and a fourth order Casimir invariant. Replacing the , indices with , and the 4 index with gives us

where is the Casimir invariant of the Lorentz algebra , and . The fourth order Casimir invariant is given by

where is the dual of the second order Casimir invariant of the Lorentz algebra, and is the Pauli-Lubanski vector from special relativity. So although our algebra is definitely 5-dimensional, it contains the familiar structures of special relativity within it.

item.title: 2: Representations
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 Representations

In the last post we looked at groups, algebras, and Casimir invariants. This time, we’re going to dive deep into the world of representation theory, and begin connecting the abstract mathematical structures of groups and algebras to the physical properties of particles in our universe.

A ‘representation’ of an algebra is a way of expressing the elements of the algebra as matrices, and the algebra operation as matrix multiplication. There are generally multiple different representations of a given algebra, and each representation can be thought of as a different way of realizing the symmetries of the algebra.

Let’s walk through the details of how to build representations using the example of the algebra , which is part of the full algebra we are looking at. First, we need to explore the structure of a bit more, and introduce the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra.

1.1 The Cartan-Weyl basis

We discussed last time that has three generators and a single Casimir invariant . The presence of a single Casimir invariant means that the representations of are classified by a single number, which we will call . This number is related to the eigenvalue of the Casimir invariant in the representation. The single Casimir invariant also means that there is a single-element subalgebra of , called the Cartan subalgebra, which is an abelian subalgebra. For the sake of definiteness we will denote it by . We can now carry out a mathematical trick, and make some more operators: and . Plugging these definitions into the commutation relations for , we find that and . This is the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra: it consists of the Cartan subalgebra , and the raising and lowering operators and , whose names will make sense shortly.

We are going to assume that the representation of we are looking at is finite-dimensional, which means that the matrices that represent the generators are finite in size. Let’s assume that we have vector space on which the generators of act. We can find a vector in this space which is an eigenvector of , so for some eigenvalue . We call the ‘weight’ of the vector . Now let’s act on with the raising operator , and measure the weight of this new vector using :

So has created a new vector with weight (hence the name ‘raising operator’). Note that the weight has increased by exactly 1: it is not a continuous change, but discrete. We could keep applying to generate vectors with higher and higher weight; but we are assuming that the representation is finite-dimensional, so at some point we must reach a vector such that . Let us call the eigenvalue of on this vector , so . We can now apply the lowering operator to generate vectors with lower and lower weight (again going down in steps of 1), until we reach a vector such that (the series must terminate because we assume a finite-dimensional representation). In the box below we prove that , and hence there are exactly vectors in the representation, with weights ranging from to in integer steps.

NoteProof that

We know two things about the vector : it has weight , so , and it is annihilated by the raising operator, so . Let’s try applying the lowering operator to , and then going back up with :

We can do something similar with an arbitrary vector which is the result of applying the lowering operator times to , so : let us first define the eigenvalue of on this vector as . Now let us consider the vector , and apply to it:

so This is an arithmetic progression: with the starting point we can solve it to find that . Now we know that (because ), so we can solve for to find that .

Now to connect to some physics. The number is called the ‘spin’ of the representation, and in order for to be an integer, can take on any non-negative half-integer value (i.e. ). This is exactly the spin associated with all the particles of the Standard Model.

1.2 Other algebras

The Cartan-Weyl basis and the method of raising and lowering operators can be applied to any simple Lie algebra, including . Because has two Casimir invariants, its representations are classified by two integers, which we can call and . The raising and lowering operators of come in pairs, and they raise and lower the weights in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of are more complicated than those of , but they can still be constructed using the same principles. The representation labels are both integers, rather than half-integers, but this is just a normalization convention: the spin of is a half-integer because we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weight by 1, whereas for we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weights by 1 in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of are important in particle physics, as they correspond to the different types of quarks and gluons in the Standard Model.

As an abelian algebra, has only one-dimensional representations, which are classified by a single integer (or half-integer) charge .

The representations of are more complicated. Because the algebra is non-compact, its representations are infinite-dimensional. We can split the two labels (as there are two Casimir invariants) into a continuous label (which will end up related to mass) and a discrete label (which will end up related to spin).

2 Casimir values

We have seen that the representations of the algebras can be labelled by a set of numbers (e.g.  for , and for ). Physically it can be more intuitive to label the representations by the values of the Casimir invariants, as these are directly related to measurable physical properties of particles and fields. We conclude this post by summarising the values of the Casimir invariants in the different representations.

For the value of the Casimir invariant in the representation with spin is given by , familiar from angular momentum in quantum mechanics. For the value of the Casimir invariant in the representation with charge is given by . For the value of the Casimir invariant in the representation with labels is given by and .

For the values of the Casimir invariants in the representation with labels are given by and . There are three different branches of representations of : a primary branch, where is a real number; a complementary branch where is pure imaginary; and a discrete series, where takes on specific values. In the next post we will connect these values to physical properties of particles in our constructed universe.

item.title: 1: The Playground
item.dc:creator: Tom Whitehead
item.description:
1 Introduction

Group field theory is a beautiful mathematical idea: that the whole of the universe emerges from the interplay of symmetries. We will begin with the simplest possible foundation: defining a set of symmetries, and then building a universe out of them. This is the playground, where we set up a series of simple rules, and then see what happens when we let them run.

The ‘group’ in ‘group field theory’ refers to a mathematical structure that captures the idea of symmetry. A group is a set of elements, along with an operation that combines two elements to produce a third. These elements represent symmetries, and the operation represents how these symmetries combine. For example, the symmetries of a square can be represented by a group consisting of rotations (by 90, 180, and 270 degrees), reflections in both the horizontal and vertical axes and through the corners, and the identity transformation (doing nothing). These symmetries can be combined in various ways: for example, a 90 degree rotation followed by a reflection across the vertical axis is equivalent to a reflection across the diagonal. The group captures all of these relationships between symmetries.

The symmetries of a square also include an important concept in group field theory: they are not commutative. This means that order in which you carry out the symmetry operations is important. For example, if you first rotate the square by 90 degrees and then reflect it across the vertical axis, you will end up in a different position than if you first reflect it and then rotate it. This non-commutativity is a key feature of the symmetries that we will be working with in group field theory, and it leads to rich and complex behavior when we start to combine these symmetries in various ways.

2 The group

Let us introduce the group that we will be using to build our universe. It is a relatively complicated group, so once we have introduced it we will break it down and explain what each component means. The group is

Let’s break it down. The first part, , is the group of symmetries of a four-dimensional spacetime with one time dimension and three spatial dimensions. The second part, , is the group of symmetries associated with electromagnetism. The third part, , is the group of symmetries associated with the weak nuclear force. The fourth part, , is the group of symmetries associated with the strong nuclear force. We have chosen this group based on observations of our universe: it was a specific choice that we made in order to build a universe that resembles our own. However, we could have chosen a different group, and we would have ended up with a different universe. This is the playground: we can choose any group we like, and then see what kind of universe emerges from it.

Equation 1 is a special type of group called a semisimple Lie group. This gives it some very nice mathematical properties that we will be using to create our universe. In particular, Lie groups have a corresponding Lie algebra, which is a mathematical structure that captures the infinitesimal symmetries of the group. The Lie algebra allows us to work with the symmetries in a more manageable way, and it will be a key tool in our construction of the universe. Each element of a Lie group can be expressed as the exponential of an element of its Lie algebra , which is a very powerful property to help us analyse the group. As the group Equation 1 is a direct product of four underlying groups, the algebra is a direct sum of the underlying algebras (compare to how normal exponentials work: ),

Each of these algebras is formed of a set of ‘generators’, which are the building blocks of the symmetries. The generators of are denoted by , where and run from 0 to 4. The generators combine together through a set of rules called the commutation relations, which capture how much the symmetries fail to commute. A commutator is defined simply as . The commutation relations for are given by

where . This looks a lot like the metric of special relativity, just with an extra spatial dimension: this will turn out not to be a coincidence!

only has a single generator, which we will denote by . The commutation relations for are trivial: (because there is only one generator, and a generator always commutes with itself). An algebra where all generators commute is called ‘abelian’.

has three generators, which we will denote by where runs from 1 to 3. The commutation relations for are given by

where is the Levi-Civita symbol, which is defined as and is antisymmetric in its indices (i.e. , and ). As not all commutation relations are zero the algebra is non-abelian. The commutation relations for are more complicated, but they have a similar structure to those of , built from generators where runs from 1 to 8.

3 Casimir invariants

A very important concept in group field theory is that of Casimir invariants. A Casimir invariant is a quantity that is constructed from the generators of the algebra, and that commutes with all of the generators. This means that it is a quantity that is conserved under the symmetries of the group. For example, for there are two Casimir invariants. The first is given by

and the second is formed using the Levi-Civita symbol again as

Next time we will investigate the physical meaning of these Casimir invariants. But let us finish this post with the Casimir invariants for the other algebras. For there is only one Casimir invariant, which is simply . For there is also only one Casimir invariant, which is given by . is again a bit more complicated: it has two Casimir invariants, which are given by

where the symmetric invariant tensor . The Casimir invariants are very important because they will turn out to be related to the physical properties of the particles in our universe. For example, the Casimir invariant will turn out to be related to the mass of particles, while will be related to the weak isospin of particles. We will dive into this correspondence between Casimir invariants and physical properties in the next post.
